mainText: Good morning. Here is your curated AI briefing for Tuesday, July 21, 2026. Google DeepMind has announced a new suite of Gemini models, representing a significant update to its efforts in providing efficient, agent-ready artificial intelligence. The release includes Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and a specialized model dubbed 3.5 Flash Cyber. According to the company, Gemini 3.6 Flash is designed as a primary workhorse model that improves upon its predecessor by reducing output token usage by 17% and offering better performance in coding and knowledge tasks. Google DeepMind cited independent data from the Artificial Analysis Index to support these efficiency claims. The 3.5 Flash-Lite model is positioned as the company’s fastest and most cost-effective option, capable of reaching 350 output tokens per second. Additionally, the new 3.5 Flash Cyber model is intended for integration with the CodeMender security agent to assist in cybersecurity workflows. Google DeepMind also noted that it has begun pre-training the next generation of its architecture, Gemini 4, and that Gemini 3.5 Pro is currently in partner testing. These models include safety safeguards specifically designed to resist jailbreaks related to chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear threats. In infrastructure developments, NVIDIA has begun the rollout of its Spectrum-6 Ethernet switch system as part of its Vera Rubin platform. This 102.4-terabit-per-second switch is designed to address the unique communication requirements of large-scale AI factories, where thousands of graphics processing units must exchange data with high synchronization. NVIDIA states that traditional Ethernet systems were not originally built for this level of collective, high-intensity traffic, and its Spectrum-X Ethernet platform aims to serve as a high-performance fabric to prevent data bottlenecks. Major infrastructure builders including Microsoft, CoreWeave, and Nebius are among the first to integrate this technology into their systems. NVIDIA emphasized that as AI models scale to the gigascale era, networking has become a critical factor in maintaining GPU utilization and overall system performance. In the music industry, streaming service Deezer reported that more than 50% of daily uploads to its platform are now AI-generated. The company stated that this volume reached an average of 90,000 tracks per day in June 2026. Deezer has been tracking this trend since January 2025, when AI-generated music accounted for only 10% of daily uploads. To manage this influx and address concerns regarding fraudulent streaming or diluted royalty payments, Deezer has implemented a policy to remove AI-generated tracks that have not received streams in the past six months. The company also noted that its internal detection technology, which identifies tracks created by tools like Suno and Udio, is available for use by other platforms, though it is currently unclear if major competitors have adopted it. In robotics, a startup named Gritt exited stealth earlier today, announcing $32 million in total funding to deploy AI-controlled robotic systems for solar energy infrastructure construction. Gritt utilizes off-the-shelf industrial hardware, such as robotic arms and skidders, combined with its own AI models to automate tasks like unloading and positioning heavy glass solar panels. The company claims that its systems allow an eight-person crew to install significantly more panels per day compared to manual labor alone. Additionally, Hugging Face released Grabette, an open-source, low-cost system designed to record robot-manipulation data without the need for expensive lab equipment. By using a handheld, camera-equipped gripper, users can record their own hand movements, which the system converts into datasets that can be used to train robotic policies. This effort aims to help developers create larger, more diverse datasets for training robots in unstructured environments. Finally, enterprise companies are continuing to shift their internal workflows toward agentic AI. Couchbase detailed an architecture using Amazon Bedrock to support multi-model deployments for its developer assistant, while Tradeshift reported that transitioning its business intelligence tools to Amazon Quick resulted in faster query response times and reduced maintenance costs. Looking ahead, market observers will be watching for the broader release of Gemini 3.5 Pro and how quickly companies like Gritt can scale their robotic operations in the field. Industry experts are also monitoring the ongoing debate regarding how music streaming services and legal systems will ultimately handle the rising volume of AI-generated content. That is your curated AI briefing for today.
